questions.question: Which war followed the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand?
questions.explanation: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary on June 28, 1914, triggered a chain reaction of declarations of war due to entangling alliances, leading to World War I. The war lasted from 1914 to 1918 and resulted in an estimated 20 million deaths.
questions.memory_hook: One assassination, two months, four empires at war — that's World War I in a nutshell.

questions.question: 'D-Day' (June 6, 1944) was the invasion of which coastline?
questions.explanation: D-Day (June 6, 1944) was the Normandy landings in Operation Overlord, the largest seaborne invasion in history. Allied forces stormed five beaches (Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, Sword) in Nazi-occupied France. It was the beginning of the liberation of Western Europe. Over 156,000 troops landed that day.
questions.memory_hook: D-Day: Normandy, June 6, 1944. Omaha Beach. The beginning of the end for Nazi Germany.

questions.question: Which tennis tournament is played on clay courts and often called 'Roland Garros'?
questions.explanation: The French Open, officially called Roland-Garros, is held at the Stade Roland Garros in Paris and played on clay courts. It is the second Grand Slam of the year (May-June). Rafael Nadal won the title a record 14 times, earning the nickname 'King of Clay.'
questions.memory_hook: Roland Garros = French Open = clay = Rafa Nadal's kingdom.

questions.question: What does 'NBA' stand for?
questions.explanation: The NBA (National Basketball Association) was formed in 1946. It is the premier professional basketball league in the world, consisting of 30 teams. The league's most prestigious individual award is the MVP (Most Valuable Player), and the championship is decided in the NBA Finals.
questions.memory_hook: National Basketball Association — the world's top basketball league, founded 1946.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Thriller' — the song, not the album?
questions.explanation: 'Thriller' the song was written by British musician Rod Temperton, who also wrote 'Rock with You' and 'Off the Wall' for Michael Jackson. Temperton, who was part of the funk band Heatwave, wrote several other hits for other artists. Jackson's Thriller album produced seven Billboard Top 10 singles.
questions.memory_hook: Rod Temperton wrote Thriller — not MJ, but the song became MJ forever.

questions.question: What film does the song 'You've Got a Friend in Me' come from?
questions.explanation: 'You've Got a Friend in Me' was written and performed by Randy Newman for the original Toy Story (1995). It has become the signature song of the Toy Story franchise and appears in all four films. Newman received an Academy Award nomination for the song and won an Oscar for 'We Belong Together' from Toy Story 3.
questions.memory_hook: Toy Story's heart: 'You've Got a Friend in Me.' Woody and Buzz, forever.

questions.question: Which singer was known as the 'Voice' and sold over 200 million records worldwide?
questions.explanation: Frank Sinatra was nicknamed 'Ol' Blue Eyes' and 'The Voice.' He sold an estimated 150–200 million records worldwide, and along with Bing Crosby, dominated popular music before rock and roll. He won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for From Here to Eternity (1953).
questions.memory_hook: Sinatra: The Voice. His way was the only way — 'My Way' said it all.

questions.question: The 'Apollo 13' mission is famous for which near-disaster?
options: Oxygen tank explosion that forced the crew to abort the Moon landing
questions.answer: Oxygen tank explosion that forced the crew to abort the Moon landing
questions.explanation: On April 13, 1970, an oxygen tank exploded on Apollo 13, crippling the spacecraft 200,000 miles from Earth. The mission to land on the Moon was aborted, and astronauts Jim Lovell, Jack Swigert, and Fred Haise used the lunar module as a lifeboat to return safely to Earth. It was called a 'successful failure.'
questions.memory_hook: 'Houston, we have a problem' — Apollo 13's oxygen tank exploded. They made it home anyway.

questions.question: Which country is home to the Great Barrier Reef?
questions.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 2,300 km along the northeast coast of Australia, in the Coral Sea. It is the world's largest coral reef system, composed of over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands. It is the largest living structure on Earth and is visible from space.
questions.memory_hook: Great Barrier Reef = Australia. 2,300 km long, visible from space, the largest living thing on Earth. Climate change and ocean warming are its biggest threats — it has experienced multiple mass bleaching events.

questions.question: What tiny organisms are responsible for producing about half of the world's oxygen?
questions.explanation: Phytoplankton are microscopic algae and cyanobacteria that float in the ocean and perform photosynthesis. They produce roughly 50-80% of Earth's oxygen — far more than all the world's forests combined. They also absorb massive amounts of carbon dioxide, making them critical to the global carbon cycle.
questions.memory_hook: Phytoplankton = half of Earth's oxygen. You breathe thanks to microscopic ocean creatures more than rainforests. The ocean, not the forest, is the planet's true lung.

questions.question: The Himalayas contain how many of the world's 14 peaks that exceed 8,000 meters in elevation?
questions.explanation: All 14 of the world's peaks that exceed 8,000 meters are located in the Himalaya-Karakoram mountain system (which is often broadly referred to as the Himalayas). The highest is Everest (8,849 m) in Nepal/Tibet, followed by K2 (8,611 m) in Pakistan/China. Mountaineers who climb all 14 are considered to have completed the 'Eight-thousanders.' The 8,000-meter threshold is equivalent to the 'death zone,' where there is not enough oxygen for the human body to sustain itself.
questions.memory_hook: All 14 peaks above 8,000m are in the Himalayas/Karakoram. Everest = 8,849m. K2 = 8,611m. The '8,000m club' of mountaineers who've climbed all 14 is tiny. Above 8,000m = 'death zone' — insufficient oxygen for human survival. Every extra meter up there is a battle against your own physiology.

questions.question: What is the term for the slow bleaching and death of coral reefs caused when rising water temperatures stress corals and cause them to expel the algae that give them color and energy?
questions.explanation: Coral bleaching occurs when ocean temperatures rise even 1-2°C above normal summer maximums, causing corals to expel the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) that live in their tissues. These algae provide up to 90% of the coral's energy and give them their color. Without them, corals turn white ('bleach') and can die if stress continues. The Great Barrier Reef has experienced mass bleaching events in 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, and 2024 — driven by climate change.
questions.memory_hook: Coral bleaching: heat stress causes corals to expel zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae) that provide 90% of their energy. Corals turn white and starve. The Great Barrier Reef has bleached 5+ times since 2016. 1-2°C above normal is enough to trigger it. Half of the world's coral has bleached since 1950.
